hypothesis: Visual signals are routed to the PFC continuously for state-monitoring regardless of context, while routing to MOs is strictly context-gated, supporting the Communication Subspaces framework of independent and target-specific routing geometries.
novelty_justification: The specific dissociation between VIS→PFC (context-invariant) and VIS→MOs (context-gated) routing geometries has not been directly measured in rodents performing a visual/auditory context-switching task, although the Communication Subspaces framework (Semedo et al., 2019) predicts target-specific routing and MacDowell et al. (2025) demonstrate multiplexed routing in related circuits.

regions.PFC: units used as prediction target (PL/ILA/ACA/ORB prefixes)

supporting.title: Cortical Areas Interact through a Communication Subspace.
supporting.authors: João Dias Semedo, A. Zandvakili, C. Machens et al.
supporting.claim: V1 and V2 interact via a low-dimensional communication subspace identified by RRR; only a subset of V1 population variance is preferentially transmitted to V2, establishing that inter-areal routing is target-specific and geometry-constrained rather than broadcast.
supporting.excerpt: Most brain functions involve interactions among multiple, distinct areas or nuclei. For instance, visual processing in primates requires the appropriate relaying of signals across many distinct cortical areas. Yet our understanding of how populations of neurons in interconnected brain areas communicate is in its infancy.

supporting.title: Multiplexed subspaces route neural activity across brain-wide networks
supporting.authors: Camden J. MacDowell, Alexandra Libby, Caroline I. Jahn et al.
supporting.claim: In mice performing flexible cognitive tasks, cortical and subcortical regions coordinate via multiplexed, task-dependent subspaces; cognitive flexibility relies on the brain routing information through different networks depending on cognitive demands, with different regions engaging different subspaces.
supporting.excerpt: Cognition is flexible, allowing behavior to change on a moment-by-moment basis. Such flexibility relies on the brain's ability to route information through different networks of brain regions to perform different cognitive computations.

supporting.title: Shifts in attention drive context-dependent subspace encoding in anterior cingulate cortex in mice during decision making
supporting.authors: M. Hajnal, Duy T. Tran, Zsombor Szabó et al.
supporting.claim: Attention shifts drive context-dependent reconfiguration of population encoding geometry in mouse anterior cingulate cortex (ACC), supporting the idea that subspace encoding changes with cognitive context in frontal areas closely related to PFC.
supporting.excerpt: Attention supports decision making by selecting the features that are relevant for decisions. Selective enhancement of the relevant features and inhibition of distractors has been proposed as potential neural mechanisms driving this selection process.

supporting.title: A Stable Population Code for Attention in Prefrontal Cortex Leads a Dynamic Attention Code in Visual Cortex
supporting.authors: A. Snyder, Byron M. Yu, M. A. Smith
supporting.claim: PFC maintains a stable population-level attention signal while visual cortex shows dynamic, stimulus-driven fluctuations; PFC precedes and leads the attention code in visual cortex, consistent with a PFC state-monitoring role that is relatively context-invariant.
supporting.excerpt: Attention often requires maintaining a stable mental state over time while simultaneously improving perceptual sensitivity. These requirements place conflicting demands on neural populations, as sensitivity implies a robust response to perturbation by incoming stimuli, which is antithetical to stability.

supporting.title: Feedforward connectivity patterns from visual areas to the front of the brain contain information about sensory stimuli regardless of awareness or report.
supporting.authors: Elise G. Rowe, Marta I. Garrido, Naotsugu Tsuchiya
supporting.claim: Feedforward input patterns from visual cortex to PFC carry stimulus-content information regardless of whether the stimulus was consciously seen or not, directly supporting context-independent VIS→PFC routing for state-monitoring.
supporting.excerpt: Unexpectedly, regardless of whether the stimulus was consciously seen or not, we found that information relating to the current sensory stimulus was present in the pattern of inputs from visual areas to the PFC.

supporting.title: Visuomotor learning promotes visually evoked activity in the medial prefrontal cortex
supporting.authors: Andrew J. Peters, Andrada-Maria Marica, J. M. Fabre et al.
supporting.claim: After visuomotor learning in mice, mPFC develops visually evoked responses that reflect learned stimulus-movement associations; this suggests mPFC continuously receives and represents visual signals once associations are learned.
supporting.excerpt: The medial prefrontal cortex (mPFC) is necessary for executing many learned associations between stimuli and movement. It is unclear, however, whether activity in the mPFC reflects sensory or motor aspects of sensorimotor associations.

supporting.title: Hippocampal-Prefrontal Communication Subspaces Align with Behavioral and Network Patterns in a Spatial Memory Task
supporting.authors: Ryan A. Young, Justin D. Shin, Ziyi Guo et al.
supporting.claim: Hippocampal-PFC communication subspaces align with both behavioral performance and network oscillatory states in a spatial memory task, demonstrating that PFC communication subspace structure is functionally meaningful and behavior-linked.
supporting.excerpt: Rhythmic network states have been theorized to facilitate communication between brain regions, but how these oscillations influence communication subspaces, i.e., the low-dimensional neural activity patterns that mediate interregional communication, and in turn how subspaces impact behavior remain unclear.

opposing.title: Attention improves information flow between neuronal populations without changing the communication subspace
opposing.authors: R. Srinath, D. Ruff, Marlene R. Cohen
opposing.claim: Spatial attention increases information flow between MT and SC without changing the geometry of the communication subspace itself; this challenges the hypothesis that context gates information routing by rotating subspace geometry, suggesting instead that routing modulation operates via gain on a fixed subspace.
opposing.excerpt: Visual attention allows observers to flexibly use or ignore visual information, suggesting that information can be flexibly routed between visual cortex and neurons involved in decision-making. We demonstrated that attention increases the rate of information flow between MT and SC, but does not change the communication subspace geometry.

opposing.title: Mechanisms underlying gain modulation in the cortex
opposing.claim: Cortical context-dependence is primarily mediated by gain modulation — multiplicative scaling of neuronal responses — rather than by geometric rotation of routing subspaces; this provides a mechanistically distinct alternative to the Communication Subspaces account.

opposing.title: Invariant neural subspaces maintained by feedback modulation
opposing.authors: L. Naumann, J. Keijser, Henning Sprekeler
opposing.claim: Context-invariant sensory representations can be established through feedback modulation that maintains stable (invariant) subspaces across contexts, rather than routing by context-gated subspace rotation; this implies the PFC-continuous pattern the hypothesis predicts might be explained by feedback stabilization rather than independent target-specific routing geometries.
opposing.excerpt: Sensory systems reliably process incoming stimuli in spite of changes in context. We show that feedforward neural networks modulated by feedback can dynamically generate invariant sensory representations.

opposing.title: Accurate Identification of Communication Between Multiple Interacting Neural Populations
opposing.authors: Belle Liu, Jacob Sacks, M. Golub
opposing.claim: Standard pairwise RRR applied to simultaneously recorded populations from multiple interacting areas produces systematically biased estimates of inter-areal communication when confounded by shared third-area inputs; the true communication subspace may be misidentified when only two of three interacting regions are modeled.
opposing.abstract: Neural recording technologies now enable simultaneous recording of population activity across many brain regions, motivating the development of data-driven models of communication between brain regions. However, existing models can struggle to disentangle the sources that influence recorded neural populations, leading to inaccurate portraits of inter-regional communication. Here, we introduce Multi-Region Latent Factor Analysis via Dynamical Systems (MR-LFADS), a sequential variational autoencoder designed to disentangle inter-regional communication, inputs from unobserved regions, and local neural population dynamics. We show that MR-LFADS outperforms existing approaches at identifying communication across dozens of simulations of task-trained multi-region networks. When applied to large-scale electrophysiology, MR-LFADS predicts brain-wide effects of circuit perturbations that were held out during model fitting. These validations on synthetic and real neural data position MR-LFADS as a promising tool for discovering principles of brain-wide information processing.

opposing.title: State-Dependent Dissociation of Shared Input and Directed Information Flow in the Visual Cortex
opposing.claim: Using laminar recordings and RRR in V1, both visual stimulation and internal brain state modulate the structure of predictive inter-laminar subspaces; this means that differences in RRR-estimated subspace usage across conditions may reflect state changes in shared input rather than directed inter-areal routing changes.
opposing.excerpt: Using laminar recordings in macaque V1, we apply reduced-rank regression to quantify low-dimensional predictive subspaces linking Input and Superficial layers. We find that both visual stimulation and internal state (eyes open vs. closed) modulate the structure.

contextual.title: Statistical methods for dissecting interactions between brain areas
contextual.authors: João Dias Semedo, Evren Gokcen, C. Machens et al.
contextual.claim: Reviews RRR and related dimensionality-reduction methods for estimating inter-areal communication subspaces from large-scale population recordings; establishes RRR as the standard estimator for this class of question.
contextual.abstract: The brain is composed of many functionally distinct areas. This organization supports distributed processing, and requires the coordination of signals across areas. Our understanding of how populations of neurons in different areas interact with each other is still in its infancy. As the availability of recordings from large populations of neurons across multiple brain areas increases, so does the need for statistical methods that are well suited for dissecting and interrogating these recordings. Here we review multivariate statistical methods that have been, or could be, applied to this class of recordings. By leveraging population responses, these methods can provide a rich description of interareal interactions. At the same time, these methods can introduce interpretational challenges. We thus conclude by discussing how to interpret the outputs of these methods to further our understanding of inter-areal interactions.

contextual.title: Reduced rank regression for neural communication: a tutorial for neuroscientists
contextual.claim: Provides a comprehensive tutorial on RRR for neural communication subspace estimation, including mathematical foundations, hyperparameter selection, and known limitations (sample size requirements, regularization sensitivity).
contextual.excerpt: Reduced rank regression (RRR) is a statistical method for finding a low-dimensional linear mapping between a set of high-dimensional inputs and outputs. In recent years, RRR has found numerous applications in neuroscience, in particular for identifying communication subspaces.

contextual.title: Measuring Stimulus Information Transfer Between Neural Populations through the Communication Subspace
contextual.claim: Proposes a mathematical framework linking communication subspace structure to the amount of stimulus information actually transmitted between populations; shared noise variability can confound subspace-based estimates of information routing.
contextual.excerpt: How this variability affects the interareal communication of sensory information is unknown. We propose a mathematical framework to understand the impact of neural population response variability on sensory information transfer.

contextual.title: Context-dependent computation by recurrent dynamics in prefrontal cortex
contextual.authors: Valerio Mante, David Sussillo, K. Shenoy et al.
contextual.claim: PFC implements context-dependent integration of sensory inputs via recurrent dynamics that selectively amplify task-relevant signals; this is the foundational evidence that PFC performs context-gated routing of sensory information, forming the backdrop against which the hypothesis's PFC-continuous claim must be evaluated.
contextual.excerpt: Prefrontal cortex is thought to have a fundamental role in flexible, context-dependent behaviour. Here we study prefrontal cortex activity in macaque monkeys trained to flexibly select and integrate noisy sensory inputs towards a choice.

contextual.title: Disentangling the flow of signals between populations of neurons
contextual.authors: Evren Gokcen, Anna Jasper, João Dias Semedo et al.
contextual.claim: DLAG (delayed latents across groups) enables disentangling bidirectional communication between neural populations, revealing that feedforward and feedback signals occupy different subspaces — a critical methodological context for interpreting pairwise RRR estimates.
contextual.excerpt: We propose here a dimensionality reduction framework, delayed latents across groups (DLAG), that disentangles signals relayed in each direction, identifies how these signals are encoded in each population.

contextual.title: Feedforward and feedback interactions between visual cortical areas use different population activity patterns
contextual.authors: João Dias Semedo, Anna Jasper, A. Zandvakili et al.
contextual.claim: Feedforward and feedback signals between V1 and V2/V3 occupy geometrically distinct population subspaces; pairwise RRR conflates both directions, potentially underestimating directional routing specificity.
contextual.excerpt: Sensory information encoded in early sensory areas is relayed to, and further processed by, higher cortical areas and then fed back. The way in which feedforward and feedback signaling interact with one another is incompletely understood.

contextual.title: Control of adaptive action selection by secondary motor cortex during flexible visual categorization
contextual.authors: Tian-Yi Wang, Jing Liu, Haishan Yao
contextual.claim: MOs (secondary motor cortex) is causally required for adaptive action selection during flexible visual categorization in mice; bilateral MOs inactivation impairs boundary switching but not perceptual discrimination, establishing MOs as a context-sensitive gate for visually-guided actions.
contextual.excerpt: Bilateral inactivation of the secondary motor cortex (M2/MOs) impairs the switch behavior, but leaves basic visual discrimination intact, suggesting MOs selectively gates context-dependent action selection.

contextual.title: Rule-based modulation of a sensorimotor transformation across cortical areas
contextual.authors: Yi-Ting Chang, E. Finkel, Duo Xu et al.
contextual.claim: In mice performing a cross-modal sensory selection task (lick to tactile or visual stimuli based on rule), rule information modulates a sensorimotor transformation across a cortical stream; the rule context reshapes population geometry along the processing chain — directly analogous to the VIS→MOs context-gating claim.
contextual.excerpt: We made single-unit recordings while head-fixed mice performed a cross-modal sensory selection task where they switched between two rules: licking in response to tactile stimuli while rejecting visual stimuli, or vice versa.

contextual.title: Transition of distinct context-dependent ensembles from secondary to primary motor cortex in skilled motor performance.
contextual.authors: Shin-ichiro Terada, Kenta Kobayashi, M. Matsuzaki
contextual.claim: Context dependency of ensemble activity is consistently high in M2/MOs layer 2/3 and low in M1, with context signals weakening from MOs to primary motor cortex; this characterizes MOs as a context-sensitive node that integrates contextual and motor signals.
contextual.excerpt: Context dependency is consistently high in M2 L2/3 neurons and consistently low in M1. Different context-dependent signals are thought to weaken from secondary to primary motor cortex.

contextual.title: Cognitive network interactions through communication subspaces in large-scale models of the neocortex
contextual.authors: Ulises Pereira-Obilinovic, S. Froudist-Walsh, Xiao-Jing Wang
contextual.claim: Large-scale cortical network models show that inter-areal communication subspaces reorganize during cognitive task switching, with different networks of areas engaging different low-dimensional routing channels depending on the cognitive context.
contextual.excerpt: Neocortex-wide neural activity is organized into distinct networks of areas engaged in different cognitive processes. We developed connectivity-constrained macaque and human whole-cortex models to elucidate the mechanism of flexible network reconfiguration.

contextual.title: Geometry of inter-areal interactions in mouse visual cortex
contextual.authors: Ramakrishnan Iyer, J. Siegle, G. Mahalingam et al.
contextual.claim: Inter-areal interaction geometry between mouse visual areas shows both fixed and dynamic components; areas interact via subspaces that partially reflect structural connectivity and partially change with stimulus and state — directly relevant to whether VIS→PFC and VIS→MOs subspaces are fixed or context-dependent.
contextual.excerpt: We aimed to study the relation between interactions among multiple areas, and if they are fixed or dynamic. The structural connectivity provides a substrate for these interactions, but anatomical connectivity is not known in sufficient detail.

contextual.title: Context-Dependent Modulation of Functional Connectivity: Secondary Somatosensory Cortex to Prefrontal Cortex Connections in Two-Stimulus-Interval Discrimination Tasks
contextual.authors: Stephanie S. Chow, R. Romo, C. Brody
contextual.claim: Functional connectivity between secondary somatosensory cortex (S2) and PFC varies with task phase (encoding vs. comparison) during a discrimination task; connectivity is context-gated in a manner analogous to what the hypothesis proposes for sensory→PFC vs. sensory→motor routing.
contextual.excerpt: In a complex world, a sensory cue may prompt different actions in different contexts. PFC neurons need to interpret the f1 information in one way and f2 information in another — a context-dependent weighting of functional connectivity.

contextual.title: Neural Mechanisms that Make Perceptual Decisions Flexible.
contextual.claim: Reviews how PFC and motor areas flexibly adjust decision-making depending on context; highlights that the mechanisms by which PFC integrates context-gated vs. continuous sensory signals remain an open question in the field.
contextual.excerpt: Neural mechanisms of perceptual decision making have been extensively studied. In contrast, we live in an ever-changing environment and have varying goals. To accommodate variability, our brain flexibly adjusts decision-making processes depending on context.

known: Inter-areal communication operates through low-dimensional subspaces identifiable by RRR: only a subset of source-area variance is transmitted to each target area (Semedo et al., 2019, Neuron; Semedo et al., 2020, Current Opinion in Neurobiology).

Subspace routing is target-specific: different downstream areas receive different projections of the same upstream population activity (Semedo et al., 2019; MacDowell et al., 2025, Nature Communications).

MOs/secondary motor cortex is causally required for context-dependent action selection during visual tasks in mice and maintains high context dependency in its population activity (Wang et al., 2019; Terada et al., 2022, Cell Reports).

PFC maintains stable population codes for cognitive states (attention, context) over time, while downstream sensory areas show more dynamic responses (Snyder et al., 2021, Journal of Neuroscience; Mante et al., 2013, Nature).
Feedforward input patterns from visual cortex to PFC carry stimulus content information regardless of awareness or task context (Rowe et al., 2023, Cortex).
unknown: Whether the VIS→PFC and VIS→MOs communication subspaces have qualitatively different context-dependence (one invariant, one gated) in rodents performing a visual/auditory context-switching task — this is the specific quantitative gap this experiment targets.

Whether RRR-estimated context modulation metrics reliably dissociate target-specific routing from shared upstream state changes: Liu et al. (2025, ICML) show that pairwise RRR is biased when a third area simultaneously interacts with both targets; the VIS-MOs-PFC three-way circuit in this experiment is precisely this scenario.

Whether the context-invariance of VIS→PFC routing is a general property or specific to certain task demands: the reviewed literature shows PFC can be context-gated (Mante et al., 2013) or context-invariant (Rowe et al., 2023) depending on the specific computation and stimulus.

The relative contribution of subspace geometry rotation versus gain modulation as the mechanism for context-dependent routing — attention studies (Srinath et al., 2021; Ferguson & Cardin, 2020) show gain modulation on a fixed subspace is a competitive alternative account.
knowledge_map.how_this_sheds_light: The specific unknown this experiment addresses is whether VIS→PFC and VIS→MOs pathways show qualitatively different context-dependence in a rodent context-switching task — a gap that sits at the intersection of the Communication Subspaces framework and the empirical literature on MOs as a context-gated node. The decision node for downstream research is whether a scientist designing a follow-up optogenetic or pharmacological experiment should target VIS→MOs routing (if strictly context-gated) versus VIS→PFC routing (if context-invariant) as the locus of contextual gating. The contribution type is Negative constraint: the null result (t=0.124, p=0.9073, N=5) does not support the hypothesized dissociation, which rules out the strongest form of the claim — that VIS→MOs context modulation is strictly larger than VIS→PFC context modulation in this dataset. A positive result would have constrained the communication architecture, providing evidence that the two downstream targets of VIS differ in their routing geometry specificity. The null result instead constrains by ruling out a large differential effect (it does not rule out a smaller differential effect given N=5). What the experiment cannot resolve even with a definitive positive or negative result: it cannot determine whether the absence of a differential effect reflects a genuine biological equivalence, a limitation of the PCA+Ridge estimator in detecting subspace-level differences, a confound from shared third-area inputs (Liu et al., 2025), or insufficient statistical power given the small N.
knowledge_map.caveat_impact: No triage caveats were flagged for this experiment. However, the dataset used (N=5 subjects, pilot cohort) has been noted in project memory as a scale limitation — the full dataset has ~114 sessions available. With N=5 subjects and per-subject mean context modulation metrics near zero (Mod_MOs=0.0128, Mod_PFC=0.0108), the test has very low power to detect the small effect sizes that would constitute evidence for or against the hypothesis. A null result at N=5 is a Negative constraint, not a Resolution: it narrows the plausible effect size range but cannot rule out a real differential effect smaller than what N=5 can detect. Scaling to the full cohort would be required to draw stronger conclusions.
